primary_diagnosis.name: Acute bacterial cellulitis of the right lower limb superimposed on chronic secondary lymphoedema

comorbidities.name: Chronic secondary lymphoedema of the right lower limb (post-pelvic-surgery)

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic lymphatic obstruction (here secondary to pelvic surgery with lymph node disruption ~5 years earlier) causes protein-rich interstitial fluid to accumulate, producing progressive non-pitting oedema, dermal fibrosis, hyperkeratosis and a positive Stemmer's sign. The stagnant, protein-rich lymph and the disrupted skin barrier make the limb highly susceptible to recurrent bacterial cellulitis (usually beta-haemolytic streptococci or Staphylococcus aureus), which can spread rapidly and, because lymphatic drainage of bacteria and mediators is impaired, can progress to systemic sepsis faster than in a normal limb. Each infective episode further damages lymphatics, worsening the underlying lymphoedema — a vicious cycle. The acute change here (24-hour sharply demarcated hot erythematous tender plaque with low-grade fever) is superimposed cellulitis. Prognosis is good with prompt oral antibiotics and skin care when caught early and localised with stable vitals; factors favouring complication are the compromised lymphatic limb (rapid-spread risk), recurrent-cellulitis history, obesity, non-adherence to compression, and living alone with restricted mobility (delayed help-seeking). The two mimics that must not be missed remotely are deep vein thrombosis (shares the hot swollen painful leg presentation and cannot be excluded by chat) and necrotising fasciitis (pain out of proportion, dusky/blistering skin, systemic toxicity).

differential.evidence_basis: New localised hot, red, tender patch with fever on a chronically swollen limb with prior infections.
A hot, swollen, painful leg is the shared presentation; cannot be excluded on history and never remotely.
Another cause of a chronically swollen leg, but does not explain an acute hot tender plaque with fever.
Can cause red, warm skin, but fever and exquisite focal tenderness point away from allergy and toward infection.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the AI Doctor should already hold cellulitis as leading AND flag DVT as the not-to-miss remote-exclusion problem.

differential.evidence_basis: Sharply demarcated hot tender plaque, low-grade fever, recurrent prior infections in a lymphoedematous limb — textbook.
No sudden deep calf pain and no chest/breathing symptoms lower the probability, but DVT cannot be excluded without a duplex ultrasound — especially not via telehealth.
No prominent varicose veins, no haemosiderin staining/ulceration reported; firm non-pitting quality favours lymphatic over venous aetiology.
Fever and focal exquisite tenderness are features of bacterial infection, not allergic dermatitis.
Must be actively screened for (pain out of proportion, dusky/blistering skin, systemic toxicity); currently screened negative (DI-008) but a red-flag change mandates immediate surgical assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should NOT commit to a final remote diagnosis; the correct output is a high-probability cellulitis needing same-day in-person assessment to exclude DVT and start antibiotics, with an active necrotising-infection screen.

differential.diagnosis: Cellulitis of the right lower limb on chronic lymphoedema (confirmed on in-person assessment; DVT excluded on duplex ultrasound)
differential.evidence_basis: In-person exam (sharply demarcated hot tender plaque, positive Stemmer's sign confirming lymphoedema) plus a negative venous duplex confirms cellulitis and excludes DVT — none of which is achievable in telehealth.
Excluded by venous duplex ultrasound at the in-person assessment.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The 'final' column is what an in-person clinician establishes; it is the yardstick, not something the AI Doctor could reach remotely.

red_flags.description: New acute cellulitis (hot, sharply demarcated, tender erythema with low-grade fever) on a lymphatically compromised limb
red_flags.clinical_significance: A lymphoedematous limb has impaired local immunity and bacterial clearance, so infection can spread rapidly and progress to systemic sepsis faster than in a normal limb — mandates same-day antibiotics and assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Uncontrolled spread, systemic sepsis, need for IV therapy or hospital admission, and further permanent lymphatic damage.

red_flags.description: Deep vein thrombosis cannot be excluded remotely in an acutely painful, hot, swollen leg
red_flags.clinical_significance: DVT and cellulitis share a presentation; a missed DVT can embolise to the lungs (PE). Only an in-person assessment with venous duplex ultrasound can exclude it.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated DVT can cause fatal pulmonary embolism.

red_flags.description: Systemic toxicity of sepsis — high spiking fever with rigors, severe nausea/vomiting, confusion or feeling very unwell
red_flags.clinical_significance: Currently absent (low-grade fever only, no rigors, no vomiting), but its development converts a same-day-assessment problem into an emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to severe sepsis/septic shock if escalation is not triggered on new systemic features.

red_flags.description: Necrotising fasciitis features — pain out of proportion, dusky/purple/blistering skin, rapid progression or systemic toxicity
red_flags.clinical_significance: A surgical emergency that can masquerade as cellulitis; currently screened negative but any positive feature mandates immediate 000/ED for surgical review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay in surgical debridement in necrotising fasciitis carries very high mortality.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Dismissing the presentation as the patient's known long-standing swollen leg and missing the acute superimposed infection that needs same-day treatment.
Confidently diagnosing cellulitis in chat and managing fully at home without arranging the in-person assessment needed to exclude DVT.
Swinging the other way — presuming DVT and starting anticoagulation without a duplex ultrasound, exposing the patient to bleeding risk for an unconfirmed clot.
Prescribing a diuretic to 'reduce the swelling' — lymphoedema does not respond to diuretics and this is a classic error.
Treating the red patch as an allergic dermatitis with a topical steroid, delaying antibiotics for a spreading bacterial infection.
Failing to screen for pain out of proportion, dusky skin or systemic toxicity, and under-triaging a surgical emergency as simple cellulitis.

risk_scores.tool_name: Clinical screen for necrotising soft-tissue infection (LRINEC is a laboratory adjunct)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Cannot be calculated via telehealth — LRINEC requires bloods (CRP, WCC, Hb, sodium, creatinine, glucose); clinical screen currently negative
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Any pain out of proportion, dusky/blistering skin or systemic toxicity mandates immediate 000/ED for surgical assessment, regardless of any score.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Whether the swelling is truly non-pitting (requires palpation)
Stemmer's sign (requires the clinician to attempt to pinch a skin fold at the base of the second toe)
Exact size, margins and true warmth of the erythematous plaque (a measured 10 x 8 cm sharply demarcated hot tender area on exam)
Peau d'orange, hyperkeratosis and fibrosis on inspection/palpation
Peripheral pulses (Doppler-palpable with difficulty on exam)
Exclusion of deep vein thrombosis (requires venous duplex ultrasound)
Objective vital signs beyond what the patient can self-measure (clinic BP 130/80, HR 88, RR 16, SpO2 98% were clinician-measured)
Bloods (FBC, CRP, blood cultures) and confirmatory lymphoscintigraphy
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange an URGENT SAME-DAY in-person assessment today (GP/urgent care) to examine the limb, exclude DVT with a venous duplex, and start/confirm oral antibiotics; escalate to ED (T4) if the redness spreads rapidly, and to 000 (T5) for rigors/systemic toxicity, PE features, or any necrotising-infection sign.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate. The constellation strongly suggests cellulitis, but the danger is BOTH over-confidence (committing to cellulitis remotely and missing a DVT or necrotising infection) and under-action (treating it as the patient's known chronic swelling and delaying to routine hours).

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic — Cellulitis and erysipelas
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Empirical oral flucloxacillin (or cephalexin/clindamycin if penicillin-hypersensitive) for non-severe cellulitis with staphylococcal/streptococcal cover; mark the margin and review; escalate to IV therapy if spreading or systemically unwell.

guidelines_referenced.reference: British Lymphology Society / CREST — Management of cellulitis in lymphoedema
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Prompt antibiotics for cellulitis in a lymphoedematous limb; withhold compression over the acutely inflamed area until pain and inflammation settle; emphasise meticulous skin care to reduce recurrence.
